content:
Member-only story

32 min read
Just now

--

When the richest company in aerospace decides a code completion tool is worth $60 Billion, more than most countries’ GDP, you know we’ve crossed into territory where the old rules don’t apply anymore.
Nuno Roberto — Chief AI and Data Architect

Press enter or click to view image in full size

I was sitting in my home office at 2:14 AM last Monday debugging a Kairos pipeline that refused to route tool calls properly through a Claude Sonnet endpoint when my phone buzzed with a Bloomberg push notification that made me set down my coffee and just stare at the screen for a solid thirty seconds. SpaceX, the rocket company, had agreed to acquire Cursor, the AI code editor, for $60 billion in an all-stock deal. Sixty. Billion. Dollars. For a company that, at its core, helps developers write code faster. I read the notification twice, checked the date to make sure it wasn’t a recycled April Fools’ joke, and then went back to my pipeline bug, because what else do you do when the world stops making the kind of sense you’re used to?
Press enter or click to view image in full size

But here’s the thing that kept nagging at me as I finally got that routing issue sorted around 4 AM: the SpaceX-Cursor deal isn’t actually insane when you look at…
